statement: Public endpoints (no auth required, or auth not gated by a paid relationship) must enforce a rate limit per IP and per identifier where applicable.
condition: An attacker, scraper, or buggy client sends requests at a rate the application cannot handle. Public signup, login, password-reset, and search endpoints are common targets.
frequency: Continuous. Public endpoints get attacked within hours of going live, even on small projects.
current_mitigation: CDN-level rate limiting (Cloudflare, Fastly), if configured.
not_fixed_because: Application-level rate limiting requires a shared store (Redis, memcached) and middleware integration.
safe_pattern: Per-IP limit at the edge (CDN or reverse proxy) for crude DDoS protection. Per-identifier limit at the application layer for misuse protection — keyed on user ID for auth'd endpoints, on email for password-reset, on session ID for search. Use a sliding-window or token-bucket implementation backed by Redis. Return HTTP 429 with a `Retry-After` header. Log rate-limit hits to a separate sink so they don't drown out the main log stream.
history: Common incident: password-reset endpoint with no rate limit. An attacker enumerated valid emails by observing response timing differences (HTTP 200 for valid, HTTP 200 for invalid but with measurably faster response). Took 6 hours to harvest a 100k-user database.
